metadata.title: Why the Missing-Values Check Ran Clean on a Broken Dataset
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Computational Skepticism candidate 04. Source chapter: books/computational-skepticism-for-ai/chapters/05-data-validation-reconstructing-the-epistemic-frame-behind-a-dataset.md
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, ink = the majority rows, terracotta = the affected subpopulation's rows, dusty blue = the model's successes, gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: ink = majority rows; terracotta #D35F43 = the affected subpopulation's rows; dusty blue #5B7B9C = the model's checks/successes; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: The chapter's opening case is a TEACHING ANECDOTE (unnamed team, composite) — treated as such: no company, no domain named on screen (FACTCHECK.md). Countable-true scale model: 100 candidate rows, 10 from the affected subpopulation; the 4% drop = exactly 4 rows fall, 3 of them from the subpopulation (3/10 of them lost vs 1/90 of the majority — the disproportion is drawn). Card exclusions: no SQL join taxonomy, no MCAR/MAR/MNAR, no six-step interrogation procedure, no imputation methods.

beats.narration_text: The exploratory analysis was spotless. Every plot drawn, every check green. And the dataset still destroyed the deployment.

card.copy: Why the missing-values check ran clean on a broken dataset
card.sub: the rows that were never there

beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] the clean check, the broken dataset

beats.narration_text: The team blamed the model. They retrained it, tuned it, swapped architectures. The failures kept coming — soft ones, in production, on patterns nobody had designed for.
beats.scene_description: a printed EDA report page pinned to newsprint — tidy histograms, a summary table, every checkbox ticked — slow push-in on the green-ticked checklist
beats.image_prompt: printed data analysis report page with small tidy histograms, a summary statistics table, and a checklist with every box ticked, pinned like a clipping to aged newsprint, desaturated flat print reproduction, editorial collage, no company names

beats.narration_text: They reread the analysis. No missing values to speak of. Reasonable distributions. No outliers. Nothing in the dataset, looked at by itself, explained anything.
beats.scene_description: the report's cleanest line set large in charcoal serif; gold highlighter sweeps 'none' as it is spoken

beats.narration_text: Here's what the analysis couldn't see. The dataset wasn't born whole. It was assembled — from three source systems, joined on a shared identifier.

production_viz.label: assembled, not born
production_viz.mechanic: three small labeled table blocks (system 1 · system 2 · system 3) draw in across the top; hairline arrows converge toward a funnel outline center-frame; a chip beneath the funnel: 'joined on a shared ID'
production_viz.note: no join-type vocabulary (card exclusion) — 'joined on a shared identifier' is the only phrase

beats.narration_text: The join had a four percent drop rate. Rows whose identifiers didn't match across all three systems fell away — silently. No error. No log. Just gone.

production_viz.label: 4% — dropped silently
production_viz.mechanic: 100 row-bars queue above the funnel (90 ink, 10 terracotta — the subpopulation visible from the start); 96 flow through into a tidy merged grid; exactly 4 deflect at the funnel lip and fall off the bottom edge; chip: '4% dropped — silently'
production_viz.note: countable-true: 100 in, 96 through, 4 fallen (3 terracotta + 1 ink)

viz.label: the 4% drop
viz.note: exact counts at the film's 100-row scale

beats.narration_text: And the drop wasn't spread evenly. One subpopulation had its identifiers formatted slightly differently, years ago, in one system. The falling rows were almost all theirs.
beats.scene_description: the end-state persists; the four fallen rows enlarge slightly at the bottom edge — three terracotta, one ink — and a chip lands: 'one subpopulation — 3 of its 10 rows, gone'

production_viz.mechanic: fallen rows re-shown enlarged; chip states the disproportion: 3 of the subpopulation's 10 lost, vs 1 of the majority's 90
production_viz.note: the disproportion is drawn and stated, not implied

beats.new_visual_element: [ANNOTATE] the fallen rows are almost all one group's

beats.narration_text: The model trained on what was present. And it performed beautifully — on what was present.

production_viz.label: beautiful — on what it saw
production_viz.mechanic: the merged 96-row grid feeds a plain 'model' box; blue ticks lag across the grid — every present row gets one; the seven missing subpopulation rows simply are not there to be wrong about
production_viz.note: blue = success; note the grid holds only 7 terracotta rows now (3 fell)

beats.narration_text: Deployed against the full population, it met the people it had never seen — and failed on exactly them. Predictably. Invisibly. In production.
beats.scene_description: a deployment stream: twelve incoming rows (nine ink, three terracotta); blue ticks land on the ink rows, terracotta X strikes land on the terracotta rows; the editor's pen rings the struck group — the film's single ring, timed to 'exactly'

production_viz.mechanic: 12-row deployment sample: 9 ink rows tick blue, 3 terracotta rows take X strikes; HandRing around the X'd group
production_viz.note: one ring only — the film's single ring

beats.new_visual_element: [ANNOTATE] the ring on the failures it never saw

beats.narration_text: And the missing-values check ran clean, because those rows weren't 'missing.' They were never there. You cannot compute the missingness of rows that never existed.

document.quote: You cannot compute the missingness of rows that never existed.

beats.scene_description: the chapter's hard lesson set large (no highlighter — the wrapped line is too wide for the bar, Gate B rule)

beats.narration_text: One question would have caught it in the first hour: why are there exactly this many rows? What was the count supposed to be?

production_viz.label: the first-hour question
production_viz.mechanic: the question sets large in serif: 'Why are there exactly N rows?' — beneath it a two-line mono ledger: 'expected  100' / 'received   96', and a gold highlighter bar sweeps the gap line: '4 rows — where?'
production_viz.note: the ledger uses the film's 100-row scale; gold = the editor's pen on the gap

beats.tts_normalized_text: One question would have caught it in the first hour: why are there exactly this many roes? What was the count supposed to be?

beats.narration_text: Data isn't born. It's assembled. And the first thing to validate isn't the values in the table — it's the rows that never reached it.

card.copy: Interrogate the row count before the rows.
card.sub: from Computational Skepticism for AI — chapter 5
